Client 2: And I want the cash-flow to feel… stable. I’m fine with growth, but not if we’re constantly tight.
Advisor: That makes sense. Let’s start high-level and then we’ll fill in details. Roughly what’s the household gross income per year right now, all-in?
Client 1: All-in household gross is 118750 annually.
Advisor: Thanks. And that’s combining your self-employed income and Linda’s pay, plus any bonus-type stuff, right?
Client 2: Yes, combined. Josep has self-employed income and also a monthly bonus stream, and I have salary.

Client 2: Yes, combined. Josep has self-employed income and also a monthly bonus stream, and I have salary.
Advisor: Great—thank you. On the spending side, what do your total monthly expenses run, just your normal household burn rate?
Client 2: Total monthly expenses are 7300 a month.
Advisor: Got it. And does that figure include the mortgage and the other loan payments?
Client 2: That includes everything we pay out monthly—mortgage, the small loan, utilities, groceries, all of it.

Client 2: That includes everything we pay out monthly—mortgage, the small loan, utilities, groceries, all of it.
Advisor: Perfect. Then just so I can separate it out, what are your monthly debt payments total—only the required payments?
Client 1: Monthly debt payments total are 2450.
Advisor: Thanks—got it. And that’s basically the mortgage plus the smaller loan, correct?
Client 1: Yeah. Mortgage is 2150 a month, and the other loan is 250 a month.

Client 1: And the other loan balance outstanding is 1100, interest rate 8.4%, final payment date 2030-09-29.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Switching gears to assets: when you think about investable assets—so brokerage and retirement accounts, not the home—what’s the total you’re working with?
Client 1: Investable assets total are 253550.
Advisor: Great. And what are the main pieces that make that up?
Client 1: I’ve got a taxable brokerage account at 144550, and there’s also a private markets sleeve on an advisor platform at 30300.

Client 1: Investable assets total are 253550.

Advisor: Okay—so staying on the month-to-month stability piece. Before we get into priorities, I want to make sure I’ve got the big debt picture right. If you add up everything you owe—mortgage plus that smaller loan—what’s the total outstanding?
Client 2: Total loans outstanding is 101150.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And that total is basically the mortgage balance plus the other loan balance, right?
Client 1: Yeah, exactly—mortgage is 100100 and the other loan is 1100.

Client 1: Yeah. Mortgage is 2150 a month, and the other loan is 250 a month.
Advisor: Helpful. While we’re on debts, do you know the rough balances and rates, just so I have the shape of it?
Client 2: Mortgage balance outstanding is 100100, interest rate is 3.89%, and the final payment date is 2031-01-16.
Client 1: And the other loan balance outstanding is 1100, interest rate 8.4%, final payment date 2030-09-29.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. Switching gears to assets: when you think about investable assets—so brokerage and retirement accounts, not the home—what’s the total you’re working with?

Advisor: Okay. And on taxes, what bracket are you generally landing in these days—do you know the band you’re in?
Client 2: We’re in the 32% bracket band.
Advisor: Thanks, Linda. That helps me not over- or under-estimate the tax drag. Switching gears—how do you want to work together going forward? Are you looking for an RIA relationship, like ongoing advice and management, or more of a one-time plan?
Client 1: We want RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Okay—thank you. Josep, when you say that, is it mainly because you want someone to actually implement and keep it on track, not just hand you a report?
